title: $TWO_PHASE_COMMIT
text: $TWO_PHASE_COMMIT Enable two-phase commit functionality. $TWO_PHASE_COMMIT Defaults Assignment file: Assignment file of the application or Uniface Server that runs that two-phase commit connector Section: [SETTINGS] Default value: None Description $TWO_PHASE_COMMIT causes Uniface to use a two-phase commit for those DBMSs which support it. Using a two-phase commit can carry a (DBMS) performance penalty, so it is advisable to use it only with those applications which require it. You do not need to assign a value to $TWO_PHASE_COMMIT . This setting should be placed in the assignment file of the application or Uniface Server that runs that two-phase commit connector, not necessarily in the client application. The client application always handles two-phase commit logic, regardless of whether the setting is locally defined. However, if there is a local database that supports two-phase commit, the setting should be defined on the client. Related Links Two-Phase Commit Physical referential integrity across more than one database (that is, distributed databases) requires a two-phase commit. This is because there is one separate commit per database (although Uniface can issue one commit to commit a path which leads to several databases). A two-phase commit allows the DBMS to ensure that modifications written to several physical locations can all be written correctly before issuing the final commit. Was this topic helpful?
